introSection: Shambhala is a steel hyper roller coaster located at PortAventura Park in Salou, Tarragona, Catalonia, Spain. The ride was built by Bolliger & Mabillard and onsite construction was handled by RCS GmbH. It opened on 12 May 2012 and was Europe's tallest coaster from opening until 2017, when Ferrari Land's Red Force opened at a height of.

sections.title: Etymology
sections.content: Shambhala is the name of a mythical kingdom used in ancient texts.

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
The ride is located in the park's China themed area and intersects with Dragon Khan, crossing it several times. The ride goes past a lake with fountains and a pedestrian pathway.

The ride has cyan rails, white track and dark grey supports.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The trains have staggered seating, first used on Behemoth at Canada's Wonderland, in 2008.

sections.title: Layout
sections.content: After leaving the station, the train turns 90 degrees to the right, then begins to climb the tall chain lift hill. Once the train reaches the top of the lift, the train drops back down to the ground reaching speeds of and passing through a tunnel. Following the first drop, the track curves upwards into a airtime hill before dropping again and racing through a sweeping high-speed ampersand-shaped helix. Following this comes some small-size camelbacks and a splash effect after a drop. The ride ends with two more camelbacks and a 180 degree turn into the station.

fullText:
Shambhala is a steel hyper roller coaster located at PortAventura Park in Salou, Tarragona, Catalonia, Spain. The ride was built by Bolliger & Mabillard and onsite construction was handled by RCS GmbH. It opened on 12 May 2012 and was Europe's tallest coaster from opening until 2017, when Ferrari Land's Red Force opened at a height of.

Shambhala is the name of a mythical kingdom used in ancient texts.

The ride is located in the park's China themed area and intersects with Dragon Khan, crossing it several times. The ride goes past a lake with fountains and a pedestrian pathway.

The ride has cyan rails, white track and dark grey supports.

The trains have staggered seating, first used on Behemoth at Canada's Wonderland, in 2008.

After leaving the station, the train turns 90 degrees to the right, then begins to climb the tall chain lift hill. Once the train reaches the top of the lift, the train drops back down to the ground reaching speeds of and passing through a tunnel. Following the first drop, the track curves upwards into a airtime hill before dropping again and racing through a sweeping high-speed ampersand-shaped helix. Following this comes some small-size camelbacks and a splash effect after a drop. The ride ends with two more camelbacks and a 180 degree turn into the station.
